file.content:
[build-system]
requires = ["pdm-backend"]
build-backend = "pdm.backend"

[project]
name = "fastapi"
dynamic = ["version"]
description = "FastAPI framework, high performance, easy to learn, fast to code, ready for production"
readme = "README.md"
license = "MIT"
license-files = ["LICENSE"]
requires-python = ">=3.10"
authors = [
    { name = "Sebastián Ramírez", email = "tiangolo@gmail.com" },
]
classifiers = [
    "Intended Audience :: Information Technology",
    "Intended Audience :: System Administrators",
    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
    "Programming Language :: Python",
    "Topic :: Internet",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Application Frameworks",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries",
    "Topic :: Software Development",
    "Typing :: Typed",
    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
    "Environment :: Web Environment",
    "Framework :: AsyncIO",
    "Framework :: FastAPI",
    "Framework :: Pydantic",
    "Framework :: Pydantic :: 2",
    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3 :: Only",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.10",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: HTTP Servers",
    "Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP",
]
dependencies = [
    "starlette>=0.46.0",
    "pydantic>=2.9.0",
    "typing-extensions>=4.8.0",
    "typing-inspection>=0.4.2",
    "annotated-doc>=0.0.2",
]

[project.urls]
Homepage = "https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi"
Documentation = "https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/"
Repository = "https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi"
Issues = "https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues"
Changelog = "https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/release-notes/"

[project.optional-dependencies]
standard = [
    "fastapi-cli[standard] >=0.0.8",
    # For the test client
    "httpx >=0.23.0,<1.0.0",
    # For templates
    "jinja2 >=3.1.5",
    # For forms and file uploads
    "python-multipart >=0.0.18",
    # To validate email fields
    "email-validator >=2.0.0",
    # Uvicorn with uvloop
    "uvicorn[standard] >=0.12.0",
    # # Settings management
    "pydantic-settings >=2.0.0",
    # # Extra Pydantic data types
    "pydantic-extra-types >=2.0.0",
]

standard-no-fastapi-cloud-cli = [
    "fastapi-cli[standard-no-fastapi-cloud-cli] >=0.0.8",
    # For the test client
    "httpx >=0.23.0,<1.0.0",
    # For templates
    "jinja2 >=3.1.5",
    # For forms and file uploads
    "python-multipart >=0.0.18",
    # To validate email fields
    "email-validator >=2.0.0",
    # Uvicorn with uvloop
    "uvicorn[standard] >=0.12.0",
    # # Settings management
    "pydantic-settings >=2.0.0",
    # # Extra Pydantic data types
    "pydantic-extra-types >=2.0.0",
]

all = [
    "fastapi-cli[standard] >=0.0.8",
    # # For the test client
    "httpx >=0.23.0,<1.0.0",
    # For templates
    "jinja2 >=3.1.5",
    # For forms and file uploads
    "python-multipart >=0.0.18",
    # For Starlette's SessionMiddleware, not commonly used with FastAPI
    "itsdangerous >=1.1.0",
    # For Starlette's schema generation, would not be used with FastAPI
    "pyyaml >=5.3.1",
    # To validate email fields
    "email-validator >=2.0.0",
    # Uvicorn with uvloop
    "uvicorn[standard] >=0.12.0",
    # Settings management
    "pydantic-settings >=2.0.0",
    # Extra Pydantic data types
    "pydantic-extra-types >=2.0.0",
]

[project.scripts]
fastapi = "fastapi.cli:main"

[dependency-groups]
dev = [
    { include-group = "tests" },
    { include-group = "docs" },
    { include-group = "translations" },
    "playwright >=1.57.0",
    "prek >=0.2.22",
]
docs = [
    { include-group = "docs-tests" },
    "black >=25.1.0",
    "cairosvg >=2.8.2",
    # for MkDocs live reload
    "click==8.2.1",
    "griffe-typingdoc >=0.3.0",
    "griffe-warnings-deprecated >=1.1.0",
    "jieba >=0.42.1",
    "markdown-include-variants >=0.0.8",
    "mdx-include >=1.4.1,<2.0.0",
    "mkdocs-macros-plugin >=1.5.0",
    "mkdocs-material >=9.7.0",
    "mkdocs-redirects >=1.2.1,<1.3.0",
    "mkdocstrings[python] >=0.30.1",
    "pillow >=11.3.0",
    "python-slugify >=8.0.4",
    "pyyaml >=5.3.1,<7.0.0",
    "typer >=0.21.1",
]
docs-tests = [
    "httpx >=0.23.0,<1.0.0",
    "ruff >=0.14.14",
    # For UJSONResponse
    "ujson >=5.8.0",
    # For ORJSONResponse
    "orjson >=3.9.3",
]
github-actions = [
    "httpx >=0.27.0,<1.0.0",
    "pydantic >=2.9.0,<3.0.0",
    "pydantic-settings >=2.1.0,<3.0.0",
    "pygithub >=2.3.0,<3.0.0",
    "pyyaml >=5.3.1,<7.0.0",
    "smokeshow >=0.5.0",
]
tests = [
    { include-group = "docs-tests" },
    "anyio[trio] >=3.2.1,<5.0.0",
    "coverage[toml] >=7.13,<8.0",
    "dirty-equals >=0.9.0",
    "flask >=3.0.0,<4.0.0",
    "inline-snapshot >=0.21.1",
    "mypy >=1.14.1",
    "pwdlib[argon2] >=0.2.1",
    "pyjwt >=2.9.0",
    "pytest >=9.0.0",
    "pytest-codspeed >=4.2.0",
    "pyyaml >=5.3.1,<7.0.0",
    "sqlmodel >=0.0.31",
    "strawberry-graphql >=0.200.0,<1.0.0",
    "ty>=0.0.9",
    "types-orjson >=3.6.2",
    "types-ujson >=5.10.0.20240515",
    "a2wsgi >=1.9.0,<=2.0.0",
    "pytest-xdist[psutil]>=2.5.0",
    "pytest-cov>=4.0.0",
    "pytest-sugar>=1.0.0",
    "pytest-timeout>=2.4.0",
]
translations = [
    "gitpython >=3.1.46",
    "pydantic-ai >=0.4.10",
    "pygithub >=2.8.1",
]

[tool.pdm]
version = { source = "file", path = "fastapi/__init__.py" }
distribution = true

[tool.pdm.build]
source-includes = [
    "tests/",
    "docs_src/",
    "scripts/",
    # For a test
    "docs/en/docs/img/favicon.png",
]

[tool.mypy]
plugins = ["pydantic.mypy"]
strict = true

[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = "fastapi.concurrency"
warn_unused_ignores = false
ignore_missing_imports = true

[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = "fastapi.tests.*"
ignore_missing_imports = true
check_untyped_defs = true

[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = "docs_src.*"
disallow_incomplete_defs = false
disallow_untyped_defs = false
disallow_untyped_calls = false

[tool.pytest]
minversion = "9.0"
addopts = [
  "--strict-config",
  "--strict-markers",
  "--ignore=docs_src",
]
strict_xfail = true
filterwarnings = [
    "error",
]
timeout = "20"

[tool.coverage.run]
parallel = true
data_file = "coverage/.coverage"
source = [
    "docs_src",
    "tests",
    "fastapi"
]
relative_files = true
context = '${CONTEXT}'
omit = [
    "tests/benchmarks/*",
    "docs_src/response_model/tutorial003_04_py39.py",
    "docs_src/response_model/tutorial003_04_py310.py",
    "docs_src/dependencies/tutorial013_an_py310.py",  # temporary code example?
    "docs_src/dependencies/tutorial014_an_py310.py",  # temporary code example?
    # Pydantic v1 migration, no longer tested
    "docs_src/pydantic_v1_in_v2/tutorial001_an_py310.py",
    "docs_src/pydantic_v1_in_v2/tutorial002_an_py310.py",
    "docs_src/pydantic_v1_in_v2/tutorial003_an_py310.py",
    "docs_src/pydantic_v1_in_v2/tutorial004_an_py310.py",
]

[tool.coverage.report]
show_missing = true
sort = "-Cover"

[tool.coverage.html]
show_contexts = true

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = [
    "E",  # pycodestyle errors
    "W",  # pycodestyle warnings
    "F",  # pyflakes
    "I",  # isort
    "B",  # flake8-bugbear
    "C4",  # flake8-comprehensions
    "UP",  # pyupgrade
]
ignore = [
    "E501",  # line too long, handled by black
    "B008",  # do not perform function calls in argument defaults
    "C901",  # too complex
    "W191",  # indentation contains tabs
]

[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]
"__init__.py" = ["F401"]
"docs_src/custom_request_and_route/tutorial002_an_py310.py" = ["B904"]
"docs_src/custom_request_and_route/tutorial002_an_py39.py" = ["B904"]
"docs_src/custom_request_and_route/tutorial002_py310.py" = ["B904"]
"docs_src/custom_request_and_route/tutorial002_py39.py" = ["B904"]

"docs_src/custom_response/tutorial007_py39.py" = ["B007"]

"docs_src/dependencies/tutorial007_py310.py" = ["F821"]
"docs_src/dependencies/tutorial007_py39.py" = ["F821"]

"docs_src/server_sent_events/tutorial001_py310.py" = ["UP028"]

[tool.ruff.lint.isort]

# Preserve types, even if a file imports `from __future__ import annotations`.
keep-runtime-typing = true

content.thinking:
Now I have all the information I need to create a detailed implementation plan. Let me design the solution carefully.

## What I Know

1. **Project structure:** FastAPI package at `/fastapi/`, tests at `/tests/`
2. **Middleware pattern:** ASGI-style class middleware with `__init__(self, app, **kwargs)` and `async __call__(self, scope, receive, send)`
3. **Existing test file:** `/tests/test_rate_limiter.py` - already written and imports `from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware`


4. **Middleware __init__.py:** Only exports `Middleware` from starlette; need to also export `RateLimitMiddleware`
5. **No existing `rate_limit.py`** - this is what we need to create

The tests show that the middleware needs to accept `requests_per_minute` and `burst_size` parameters, allowing up to the burst size before enforcing the rate limit, then blocking subsequent requests until the window resets. 5 requests (burst_size), 6th is 429 with `{"detail": "Rate limit exceeded...", "retry_after": <float>}`
3. `test_rate_limit_headers_present`: All responses have `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
4. `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases`: `X-RateLimit-Remaining` decreases
5. `test_rate_limit_per_ip`: `X-RateLimit-Limit` == "60"
6. `test_different_paths_share_limit`: Rate limit applies across all paths (3 from /, 2 from /other = 5 total = blocked)
7. `test_429_response_format`: JSON with `detail` (str), `retry_after` (int or float, > 0)
8. `test_custom_limits`: With `requests_per_minute=120, burst_size=2` - only 2 requests succeed, 3rd is 429

Now I'm thinking through the token bucket algorithm implementation. Each IP gets a bucket starting with `burst_size` tokens that refill at a rate of `requests_per_minute / 60` tokens per second, and each request consumes one token. I need to track the current token count and last refill timestamp per IP, then calculate elapsed time since the last refill to determine how many new tokens to add (capped at the burst size).

Implementing the request handling logic... Looking at the test expectations, `X-RateLimit-Limit` should be the string representation of `requests_per_minute`, and `X-RateLimit-Remaining` needs to be an integer that decreases with each request. I also need to handle thread safety for the token bucket since this middleware runs in an async context, so I'll need a lock mechanism per IP address. I'm going in circles here. The core issue is that the token bucket logic itself has no awaits, so it runs atomically within a coroutine thanks to the GIL, but for true thread-safety across multiple workers I'd need a lock. Since the tests are synchronous and single-threaded, a simple dict approach will work fine for now, though I could add `threading.Lock` for extra safety or upgrade to `asyncio.Lock` later if needed.

Now I need to figure out how to add the rate limit headers to the response—the middleware receives `scope, receive, send` at the ASGI level, so I'll need to wrap the `send` callable to intercept and modify the response headers. I'm wrapping the send callable to inject rate limit headers into the response, and for rate limit violations I'm using Starlette's JSONResponse to return a 429 status with retry information. Now I'm extracting the client IP from the ASGI scope and planning the implementation structure with a token bucket class to track rate limit state per client.

Defining the token bucket class... Writing the token bucket implementation... Writing the rate limit handler... Now I'm calling the app with the modified send function that adds rate limit headers to responses. But I'm realizing there's a potential threading issue—I'm acquiring the lock twice in sequence when getting or creating a bucket and then consuming tokens, which should be fine since they're separate lock acquisitions rather than nested ones. Though I need to double-check the retry_after calculation when the bucket denies the request, since the token count hasn't actually been decremented at that point.

Refactoring the consume method... I'm checking the test cases to verify my response format matches what's expected—the 429 response needs to include both a "detail" message and a "retry_after" value, which my implementation already provides.

Now I'm working through the token bucket math to ensure `retry_after` is always positive. With a burst of 5 requests and a refill rate of 1 token per second, after exhausting the burst on the 6th request, the calculation should yield around 1.0 second, which satisfies the test requirement. Though I need to account for the possibility that tokens might refill slightly during rapid test execution, so I should trace through the timing more carefully. The 6th request gets blocked since tokens are depleted and the refill rate can't keep up, returning a retry_after of about 1 second. For the custom limits test with a higher refill rate of 2 tokens per second, the first two requests consume the burst capacity and the third gets blocked as expected. Now I'm checking the shared limit scenario where multiple paths draw from the same token bucket.

Looking at the headers test, it only validates the rate limit headers on successful responses, but doesn't explicitly verify they're present on 429 responses—which would be good practice to include.

Now for the middleware export, the task asks to export it from the package, but the test imports directly from the rate_limit module. I need to check whether adding it to the `__init__.py` is the right approach or if the current import path is what's expected.

Looking at the thread safety, I'm realizing there's a potential race condition between getting or creating the bucket and consuming from it. Even though `consume` is protected by the lock, the bucket retrieval happens outside of it, which could cause issues if two threads try to access the same bucket simultaneously. I need to ensure the entire operation—both getting the bucket and consuming from it—happens atomically within the lock. Now I'm thinking about the type annotations for mypy strict mode—I need to properly type the buckets dictionary and the send wrapper. The `send` parameter from Starlette is a callable that takes a Message (a mutable mapping) and returns an awaitable, so I'll type the wrapper accordingly. For adding headers to the response, I'm using MutableHeaders from Starlette's datastructures to modify the message when the type is "http.response.start".

Now I need to finalize the implementation plan: creating the new rate limit middleware file and updating the middleware exports. The existing test file should pass once the middleware is implemented. responses with rate limit headers, maintain thread safety with locking, follow ASGI middleware patterns by wrapping the send callable, and ensure everything is fully type-annotated for strict mypy compliance.

I need to design a token-bucket rate limiting middleware for a FastAPI codebase. Here is all the context you need:

## Project Context
- **Location:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471883401-vhyaxc/`
- Python >=3.10, FastAPI on Starlette, mypy strict mode, ruff linting
- Tests use pytest, `TestClient` from `fastapi.testclient`
- No external async locking libs — only stdlib

## Key Files
- `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` — currently just: `from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware`
- `fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py` — reference custom middleware (ASGI class-based pattern)
- `fastapi/middleware/cors.py` — example: `from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware as CORSMiddleware  # noqa`
- `tests/test_rate_limiter.py` — **already exists**, tests we must pass

## Existing Test File (verbatim)
```python
import time
import pytest
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware

@pytest.fixture
def app():
    app = FastAPI()
    app.add_middleware(
        RateLimitMiddleware,
        requests_per_minute=60,
        burst_size=5,
    )
    @app.get("/")
    async def root():
        return {"message": "hello"}
    @app.get("/other")
    async def other():
        return {"message": "other"}
    return app

@pytest.fixture
def client(app):
    return TestClient(app)

class TestRateLimitMiddleware:
    def test_allows_requests_under_limit(self, client):
        for _ in range(5):
            response = client.get("/")
            assert response.status_code == 200

    def test_blocks_requests_over_burst(self, client):
        for _ in range(5):
            client.get("/")
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 429
        body = response.json()
        assert "Rate limit exceeded" in body["detail"]
        assert "retry_after" in body

    def test_rate_limit_headers_present(self, client):
        response = client.get("/")
        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
        assert "X-RateLimit-Remaining" in response.headers
        assert "X-RateLimit-Reset" in response.headers

    def test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases(self, client):
        r1 = client.get("/")
        remaining1 = int(r1.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
        r2 = client.get("/")
        remaining2 = int(r2.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
        assert remaining2 < remaining1

    def test_rate_limit_per_ip(self, client):
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 200
        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
        assert response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "60"

    def test_different_paths_share_limit(self, client):
        for _ in range(3):
            client.get("/")
        for _ in range(2):
            client.get("/other")
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 429

    def test_429_response_format(self, client):
        for _ in range(5):
            client.get("/")
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 429
        body = response.json()
        assert isinstance(body["detail"], str)
        assert isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))
        assert body["retry_after"] > 0

    def test_custom_limits(self):
        app = FastAPI()
        app.add_middleware(
            RateLimitMiddleware,
            requests_per_minute=120,
            burst_size=2,
        )
        @app.get("/")
        async def root():
            return {"ok": True}
        client = TestClient(app)
        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
        assert client.get("/").status_code == 429
```

## Custom Middleware Pattern (asyncexitstack.py)
```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

## Requirements
1. **Token bucket algorithm** — per-client IP
2. **Configurable:** `requests_per_minute` and `burst_size` parameters
3. **429 with retry_after** when limit exceeded — JSON body: `{"detail": "Rate limit exceeded...", "retry_after": <seconds>}`
4. **X-RateLimit-* headers on all responses:** `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
5. Must be a pure ASGI low-level middleware (NOT BaseHTTPMiddleware) using `scope/receive/send` pattern
6. Must use stdlib only (no new deps)
7. Must export from `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`
8. Mypy strict mode must pass — full type annotations required

## Critical Design Constraints

**ASGI send wrapping**: To inject response headers into ALL responses (not just 429), we need to intercept the `send` callable to inject headers into `http.response.start` messages. This is the only way to add headers in pure ASGI without BaseHTTPMiddleware.

**Thread safety**: TestClient is sync (uses anyio in a thread), so the in-memory token bucket dict may be accessed "concurrently". Use `threading.Lock` for the bucket dict (since this is synchronous in tests and the send wrapping is also synchronous).

**Token Bucket Algorithm:**
- Each IP has a bucket with `tokens` (float) and `last_refill` (float timestamp)
- On each request: refill tokens based on elapsed time since last refill at rate `requests_per_minute / 60` tokens/second, capped at `burst_size`
- If tokens >= 1: consume 1 token and allow; else reject with 429
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining` = `int(tokens)` after consuming (or 0 if blocked)
- `X-RateLimit-Reset` = Unix timestamp when bucket will have 1 token: `now + (1 - tokens) / refill_rate`
- `retry_after` = seconds until next token is available

**IP extraction**: `scope["client"][0]` if `scope.get("client")` else `"unknown"`

**Non-HTTP requests**: Pass through immediately without rate limiting (websockets etc.)

Please design the exact implementation with:
1. Complete token bucket dataclass/class
2. Complete `RateLimitMiddleware` class with all methods and type annotations
3. How to update `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`
4. Verify the design satisfies every test case

Return a detailed, step-by-step implementation plan.

12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54

I have all the information needed. Let me now trace through each test case carefully to verify the algorithm design.

**Test analysis:**

- `test_allows_requests_under_limit`: 5 requests with `burst_size=5` all return 200. This means when a bucket starts, it has exactly `burst_size` tokens (5). After each consume, tokens decrease. All 5 must succeed.
- `test_blocks_requests_over_burst`: After 5 requests, the 6th returns 429. Tokens start at 5, after 5 consumes = 0.0 tokens, so 6th fails.
- `test_rate_limit_headers_present`: All responses (200 and 429) have the three headers.
- `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases`: First request gets `remaining1`, second gets `remaining2 < remaining1`. Starting at 5 tokens: first consumes → 4 remaining, second → 3. So `remaining` = floor(tokens after consume).
- `test_rate_limit_per_ip`: `X-RateLimit-Limit` equals `"60"` (the `requests_per_minute` value).
- `test_different_paths_share_limit`: 3 on `/` + 2 on `/other` = 5 total, then 6th fails. Rate limit is per-IP across all paths.
- `test_custom_limits`: `burst_size=2`, requests 1 and 2 succeed, 3rd fails.

**Token counting precision for `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases`:**

With `burst_size=5` and initial tokens=5.0:
- Request 1: refill (no elapsed time, tokens stays 5.0), consume 1 → tokens=4.0, `remaining = int(4.0) = 4`
- Request 2: tiny elapsed time → refill barely anything, tokens≈4.0+epsilon, consume 1 → tokens≈3.0, `remaining = int(3.0) = 3`

So `remaining2 (3) < remaining1 (4)`. This works correctly.

**Critical edge: `test_custom_limits` with `burst_size=2`:**

Request 1: tokens=2.0, consume → 1.0 remaining (200 OK)
Request 2: tokens≈1.0 (tiny refill), consume → 0.0 (200 OK, tokens >= 1 at time of check)
Request 3: tokens≈0.0 (tiny refill, but < 1), reject 429

Wait — for request 2 to succeed, we need `tokens >= 1.0` BEFORE consuming. After consuming request 1, tokens = 1.0. For request 2, there's a tiny elapsed time so tokens = 1.0 + tiny_amount. Since 1.0 + tiny >= 1, it passes and tokens → ~0.0+tiny.

For request 3: tokens ≈ 0.0 + another_tiny < 1.0. Blocked. Correct.

**The `retry_after` calculation:**

When tokens < 1, we need `retry_after = (1 - tokens) / refill_rate`. With `refill_rate = requests_per_minute / 60.0` tokens/second.

The test requires `retry_after > 0`. When tokens = 0 exactly after burst is fully depleted: `(1 - 0) / (60/60) = 1.0` second. Good.

**ASGI `send` wrapping pattern:**

The key insight is that for a 429 response, we bypass `self.app` entirely and send our own response. For allowed requests, we wrap `send` to intercept `http.response.start` and inject rate-limit headers. This is exactly how pure ASGI middleware works.

**Threading concern:**

`TestClient` uses `anyio` in a thread (synchronous test code in separate thread, async event loop in the background). All requests in a single test are sequential (synchronous test). The `threading.Lock` is a correct choice — it protects the `_buckets` dict from concurrent access between requests.

Now let me verify the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header calculation. Reset = timestamp when bucket will have 1 token:

- If `tokens >= 1` after refill (allowed request): reset = `now + (1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate`. But `tokens_after_consume = tokens - 1`. So `reset = now + (1 - (tokens-1)) / refill_rate = now + (2 - tokens) / refill_rate`.
  
  Actually, simpler: Reset = time when next token arrives after current state. After consuming, tokens = T - 1. Time to reach 1 token again = `(1 - (T-1)) / refill_rate = (2 - T) / refill_rate`. If T = 5 (full), `reset = now + (2-5)/rate` which is negative — that means reset is in the past (bucket is not depleted). In that case, reset = `now` (already have tokens).

Let me reconsider. The `X-RateLimit-Reset` semantics: "Unix timestamp when the rate limit resets (when next request will be allowed)". The cleaner interpretation:

- If `tokens_after_consume >= 1`: reset = `now` (you can already make another request). But that's odd.
- Better: Reset = unix timestamp when the bucket will be at `burst_size` (full). That would be `now + (burst_size - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate`.
- Or: reset = when there will be at least 1 token if not yet: `max(now, now + (1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate)`.

The test only checks that the header EXISTS and is parseable as a string — it doesn't validate the value content. So any reasonable timestamp works. The description says: "Unix timestamp when bucket will have 1 token: `now + (1 - tokens) / refill_rate`" where `tokens` is the current state (post-consume).

If `tokens_after_consume >= 1`: `(1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate` is negative or zero, so `reset = now + negative = past`. It's cleaner to clamp: `reset = max(now, now + (1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate)`.

For the 429 case: tokens < 1, `(1 - tokens) / refill_rate > 0`, so `retry_after = (1 - tokens) / refill_rate` and `reset = now + retry_after`. Both are positive. The test checks `body["retry_after"] > 0`. Good.

Now I have a complete picture. Here is the full implementation plan:

## Implementation Plan: Token-Bucket Rate Limiting Middleware

### Overview

Two deliverables:
1. New file: `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py` — contains `TokenBucket` dataclass and `RateLimitMiddleware` ASGI class.
2. Update: `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` — add export of `RateLimitMiddleware`.

### Step 1: `TokenBucket` Dataclass

File: `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py`

```python
import dataclasses

@dataclasses.dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float  # monotonic or wall-clock timestamp from time.monotonic()
```

Use `time.monotonic()` for refill timing (immune to system clock adjustments). Use `time.time()` only for the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header value (Unix timestamp, as tests would expect a real-world timestamp).

The dataclass needs no methods — all logic lives in the middleware. This keeps the bucket a pure data container.

### Step 2: `RateLimitMiddleware` Class — Full Structure

```python
import dataclasses
import json
import threading
import time
from collections.abc import Awaitable, Callable
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Message, Receive, Scope, Send

@dataclasses.dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float

class RateLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self,
        app: ASGIApp,
        requests_per_minute: int = 60,
        burst_size: int = 10,
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.requests_per_minute = requests_per_minute
        self.burst_size = burst_size
        self.refill_rate: float = requests_per_minute / 60.0  # tokens per second
        self._buckets: dict[str, TokenBucket] = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def _get_client_ip(self, scope: Scope) -> str:
        client = scope.get("client")
        if client is not None:
            return str(client[0])
        return "unknown"

    def _consume_token(self, ip: str) -> tuple[bool, float, float]:
        """
        Returns (allowed, tokens_remaining, retry_after_seconds).
        tokens_remaining is after consuming (or current if blocked).
        retry_after_seconds is 0.0 if allowed.
        Thread-safe via self._lock.
        """
        ...

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        ...
```

### Step 3: `_consume_token` Method — Detailed Logic

This is the thread-safe token bucket implementation:

```python
def _consume_token(self, ip: str) -> tuple[bool, float, float]:
    now_mono = time.monotonic()
    with self._lock:
        if ip not in self._buckets:
            # New client: start with full burst
            self._buckets[ip] = TokenBucket(
                tokens=float(self.burst_size),
                last_refill=now_mono,
            )
        bucket = self._buckets[ip]

        # Refill: add tokens based on elapsed time
        elapsed = now_mono - bucket.last_refill
        bucket.tokens = min(
            float(self.burst_size),
            bucket.tokens + elapsed * self.refill_rate,
        )
        bucket.last_refill = now_mono

        if bucket.tokens >= 1.0:
            bucket.tokens -= 1.0
            tokens_remaining = bucket.tokens
            return True, tokens_remaining, 0.0
        else:
            # Blocked: calculate when next token arrives
            retry_after = (1.0 - bucket.tokens) / self.refill_rate
            return False, bucket.tokens, retry_after
```

**Why this is correct for the tests:**

- New bucket starts at `burst_size` tokens. First consume: `5.0 - 1 = 4.0` remaining. Second: `4.0 + tiny - 1 ≈ 3.0`. Monotonically decreasing. `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases` passes.
- After 5 consumes from 5.0 starting tokens: tokens ≈ 0.0. 6th request: tokens < 1.0, blocked. `test_blocks_requests_over_burst` passes.
- `burst_size=2`: first consume from 2.0 → 1.0 (pass). Second: 1.0 + epsilon - 1 ≈ 0.0+epsilon (pass, since >= 1.0 before consume). Third: epsilon < 1.0, blocked. `test_custom_limits` passes.

**Critical subtlety for `burst_size=2`:** After request 1, `tokens = 1.0`. At request 2, elapsed time is nanoseconds (TestClient is sync-sequential). `tokens = min(2.0, 1.0 + elapsed * 2.0)`. Since `elapsed ≈ 1e-5` seconds and `refill_rate = 2.0 tokens/sec`, `elapsed * 2.0 ≈ 2e-5`. So `tokens ≈ 1.00002`. Since `1.00002 >= 1.0`, consume succeeds: `tokens = 0.00002`. Request 3: `tokens = 0.00002 + tiny < 1.0`, blocked. Correct.

### Step 4: `__call__` Method — ASGI Handler

```python
async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
    # Pass through non-HTTP connections (websockets, lifespan, etc.)
    if scope["type"] != "http":
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
        return

    ip = self._get_client_ip(scope)
    allowed, tokens_remaining, retry_after = self._consume_token(ip)

    # Precompute headers shared by both allowed and blocked responses
    reset_timestamp = time.time() + retry_after  # For allowed: retry_after=0 → reset=now
    limit_header = str(self.requests_per_minute)
    remaining_header = str(int(tokens_remaining))
    reset_header = str(int(reset_timestamp))

    if not allowed:
        # Short-circuit: send 429 directly without calling self.app
        await self._send_429(send, retry_after, limit_header, remaining_header, reset_header)
        return

    # Wrap send to inject rate-limit headers into the downstream response
    async def send_with_headers(message: Message) -> None:
        if message["type"] == "http.response.start":
            # Inject headers into the response start message
            headers: list[tuple[bytes, bytes]] = list(message.get("headers", []))
            headers.extend([
                (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
                (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
                (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
            ])
            message = {**message, "headers": headers}
        await send(message)

    await self.app(scope, receive, send_with_headers)
```

**Note on `message` mutation:** We construct a new dict `{**message, "headers": headers}` rather than mutating the original message. This is safer and avoids aliasing issues. The `message` type is `MutableMapping[str, Any]` in Starlette, so this spread pattern produces a plain `dict`, which is acceptable.

**Note on `reset_header` for allowed requests:** When `retry_after = 0`, `reset = time.time() + 0 = time.time()`. This gives the current Unix timestamp. The test only checks that the header is present, not its value content. This is semantically: "the rate limit resets now / you're not depleted".

A slightly better semantic for `X-RateLimit-Reset` for allowed requests would be the time when the bucket will be completely refilled, but since the test only checks for header presence, the simple approach is fine.

### Step 5: `_send_429` Helper Method

```python
async def _send_429(
    self,
    send: Send,
    retry_after: float,
    limit_header: str,
    remaining_header: str,
    reset_header: str,
) -> None:
    body_data = {
        "detail": f"Rate limit exceeded. Try again in {retry_after:.1f} seconds.",
        "retry_after": retry_after,
    }
    body = json.dumps(body_data).encode("utf-8")

    headers = [
        (b"content-type", b"application/json"),
        (b"content-length", str(len(body)).encode()),
        (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
        (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
        (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
    ]

    await send({
        "type": "http.response.start",
        "status": 429,
        "headers": headers,
    })
    await send({
        "type": "http.response.body",
        "body": body,
        "more_body": False,
    })
```

**Test verification for `test_blocks_requests_over_burst`:**
- `body["detail"]` contains `"Rate limit exceeded"` — satisfied by the format string.
- `"retry_after" in body` — key exists.
- `body["retry_after"] > 0` — with `tokens ≈ 0.0`, `retry_after = 1.0 / 1.0 = 1.0 > 0`. Satisfied.
- `isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))` — `json.dumps` of a Python `float` produces a JSON number; `response.json()` parses it back to `float`. Satisfied.

### Step 6: Complete Type Annotations for mypy Strict Mode

The complete import block and type annotations:

The `send_with_headers` inner function has the signature `async def send_with_headers(message: Message) -> None`. `Message` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]` in Starlette's types.

The `_consume_token` return type is `tuple[bool, float, float]`.

The `_buckets` dict type is `dict[str, TokenBucket]`.

For the `{**message, "headers": headers}` pattern, mypy may complain because `Message` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]`, not directly spreadable as `**`. The safer approach is:

```python
new_message["headers"] = headers
await send(new_message)
```

Since `Message` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]`, `dict(message)` returns `dict[str, Any]` which is a valid `MutableMapping[str, Any]` and thus a valid `Message`.

### Complete File: `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py`

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import dataclasses
import json
import threading
import time

from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Message, Receive, Scope, Send


@dataclasses.dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float


class RateLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self,
        app: ASGIApp,
        requests_per_minute: int = 60,
        burst_size: int = 10,
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.requests_per_minute = requests_per_minute
        self.burst_size = burst_size
        self.refill_rate: float = requests_per_minute / 60.0
        self._buckets: dict[str, TokenBucket] = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def _get_client_ip(self, scope: Scope) -> str:
        client = scope.get("client")
        if client is not None:
            return str(client[0])
        return "unknown"

    def _consume_token(self, ip: str) -> tuple[bool, float, float]:
        now = time.monotonic()
        with self._lock:
            if ip not in self._buckets:
                self._buckets[ip] = TokenBucket(
                    tokens=float(self.burst_size),
                    last_refill=now,
                )
            bucket = self._buckets[ip]
            elapsed = now - bucket.last_refill
            bucket.tokens = min(
                float(self.burst_size),
                bucket.tokens + elapsed * self.refill_rate,
            )
            bucket.last_refill = now

            if bucket.tokens >= 1.0:
                bucket.tokens -= 1.0
                return True, bucket.tokens, 0.0
            else:
                retry_after = (1.0 - bucket.tokens) / self.refill_rate
                return False, bucket.tokens, retry_after

    async def _send_429(
        self,
        send: Send,
        retry_after: float,
        limit_header: str,
        remaining_header: str,
        reset_header: str,
    ) -> None:
        body_data = {
            "detail": (
                f"Rate limit exceeded. Try again in {retry_after:.1f} seconds."
            ),
            "retry_after": retry_after,
        }
        body = json.dumps(body_data).encode("utf-8")
        headers: list[tuple[bytes, bytes]] = [
            (b"content-type", b"application/json"),
            (b"content-length", str(len(body)).encode()),
            (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
            (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
            (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
        ]
        await send(
            {
                "type": "http.response.start",
                "status": 429,
                "headers": headers,
            }
        )
        await send(
            {
                "type": "http.response.body",
                "body": body,
                "more_body": False,
            }
        )

    async def __call__(
        self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send
    ) -> None:
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return

        ip = self._get_client_ip(scope)
        allowed, tokens_remaining, retry_after = self._consume_token(ip)

        reset_time = time.time() + retry_after
        limit_header = str(self.requests_per_minute)
        remaining_header = str(int(tokens_remaining))
        reset_header = str(int(reset_time))

        if not allowed:
            await self._send_429(
                send,
                retry_after,
                limit_header,
                remaining_header,
                reset_header,
            )
            return

        async def send_with_headers(message: Message) -> None:
            if message["type"] == "http.response.start":
                new_message: Message = dict(message)
                existing: list[tuple[bytes, bytes]] = list(
                    new_message.get("headers", [])
                )
                existing.extend(
                    [
                        (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
                        (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
                        (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
                    ]
                )
                new_message["headers"] = existing
                await send(new_message)
            else:
                await send(message)

        await self.app(scope, receive, send_with_headers)
```

### Complete Update: `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`

```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware

from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware as RateLimitMiddleware
```

---

### Edge Cases and Design Notes

**Why `time.monotonic()` for bucket timing and `time.time()` for reset header:**
`time.monotonic()` is immune to NTP adjustments and never goes backward. It's correct for computing elapsed intervals. `time.time()` gives a Unix timestamp, which is what the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header convention expects (a wall-clock epoch time).

**Why `threading.Lock` not `asyncio.Lock`:**
Starlette's `TestClient` runs the ASGI app in a thread with `anyio`. The `_consume_token` method is called from within the async `__call__` coroutine, but since the lock is only held briefly (pure computation, no I/O), it's safe to use `threading.Lock` even in async context. An `asyncio.Lock` would fail across threads and event loops.

**Why no `asyncio.Lock` wrapping the bucket dict:**
The `_consume_token` method is synchronous and holds the lock for microseconds. This is appropriate for in-process rate limiting with stdlib-only constraint. For production async code, an `asyncio.Lock` per-IP would be better, but the requirement prohibits external async locking libs and the test environment is synchronous.

**Why headers are lowercase (`x-ratelimit-*`) in the raw ASGI layer:**
HTTP/2 headers must be lowercase. HTTP/1.1 headers are case-insensitive. Starlette's response handling normalizes headers, but providing them lowercase is the correct convention for ASGI. The `TestClient` (httpx-based) normalizes header names when accessed via `response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"]` (case-insensitive lookup), so tests will pass regardless.

**Why `more_body: False` in the body message:**
This signals to the ASGI server that the response body is complete. Without it, some servers may hang waiting for more body data.

**Why `dict(message)` rather than `message` mutation:**
The `message` dict may be shared or reused by the upstream ASGI server. Constructing a new dict prevents aliasing bugs.

```python
import time
import pytest
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware

@pytest.fixture
def app():
    app = FastAPI()
    app.add_middleware(
        RateLimitMiddleware,
        requests_per_minute=60,
        burst_size=5,
    )

    async def root():
        return {"message": "hello"}
    @app.get("/other")
    async def other():
        return {"message": "other"}

@pytest.fixture
def client(app):
    return TestClient(app)

class TestRateLimitMiddleware:
    def test_allows_requests_under_limit(self, client):
        for _ in range(5):
            response = client.get("/")
            assert response.status_code == 200

    def test_blocks_requests_over_burst(self, client):
        for _ in range(5):
            client.get("/")
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 429
        body = response.json()
        assert "Rate limit exceeded" in body["detail"]
        assert "retry_after" in body

    def test_rate_limit_headers_present(self, client):
        response = client.get("/")
        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
        assert "X-RateLimit-Remaining" in response.headers
        assert "X-RateLimit-Reset" in response.headers

    def test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases(self, client):
        r1 = client.get("/")
        remaining1 = int(r1.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
        r2 = client.get("/")
        remaining2 = int(r2.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
        assert remaining2 < remaining1

    def test_rate_limit_per_ip(self, client):
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 200
        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
        assert response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "60"

        for _ in range(3):
            client.get("/")
        for _ in range(2):
            client.get("/other")
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 429

    def test_429_response_format(self, client):
        for _ in range(5):
            client.get("/")
        response = client.get("/")
        assert response.status_code == 429
        body = response.json()
        assert isinstance(body["detail"], str)
        assert isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))
        assert body["retry_after"] > 0

    def test_custom_limits(self):
        app = FastAPI()
        app.add_middleware(
            RateLimitMiddleware,
            requests_per_minute=120,
            burst_size=2,
        )
        @app.get("/")
        async def root():
            return {"ok": True}
        client = TestClient(app)
        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
        assert client.get("/").status_code == 429
```

## Custom Middleware Pattern (asyncexitstack.py)
```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

## Requirements
1. **Token bucket algorithm** — per-client IP
2. **Configurable:** `requests_per_minute` and `burst_size` parameters
3. **429 with retry_after** when limit exceeded — JSON body: `{"detail": "Rate limit exceeded...", "retry_after": <seconds>}`
4. **X-RateLimit-* headers on all responses:** `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
5. Must be a pure ASGI low-level middleware (NOT BaseHTTPMiddleware) using `scope/receive/send` pattern
6. Must use stdlib only (no new deps)
7. Must export from `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`
8. Mypy strict mode must pass — full type annotations required

## Critical Design Constraints

**ASGI send wrapping**: To inject response headers into ALL responses (not just 429), we need to intercept the `send` callable to inject headers into `http.response.start` messages. This is the only way to add headers in pure ASGI without BaseHTTPMiddleware.

**Thread safety**: TestClient is sync (uses anyio in a thread), so the in-memory token bucket dict may be accessed "concurrently". Use `threading.Lock` for the bucket dict (since this is synchronous in tests and the send wrapping is also synchronous).

**Token Bucket Algorithm:**
- Each IP has a bucket with `tokens` (float) and `last_refill` (float timestamp)
- On each request: refill tokens based on elapsed time since last refill at rate `requests_per_minute / 60` tokens/second, capped at `burst_size`
- If tokens >= 1: consume 1 token and allow; else reject with 429
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining` = `int(tokens)` after consuming (or 0 if blocked)
- `X-RateLimit-Reset` = Unix timestamp when bucket will have 1 token: `now + (1 - tokens) / refill_rate`
- `retry_after` = seconds until next token is available

**IP extraction**: `scope["client"][0]` if `scope.get("client")` else `"unknown"`

**Non-HTTP requests**: Pass through immediately without rate limiting (websockets etc.)

Please design the exact implementation with:
1. Complete token bucket dataclass/class
2. Complete `RateLimitMiddleware` class with all methods and type annotations
3. How to update `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`
4. Verify the design satisfies every test case

Return a detailed, step-by-step implementation plan.

I have all the information needed. Let me now trace through each test case carefully to verify the algorithm design.

**Test analysis:**

- `test_allows_requests_under_limit`: 5 requests with `burst_size=5` all return 200. This means when a bucket starts, it has exactly `burst_size` tokens (5). After each consume, tokens decrease. All 5 must succeed.
- `test_blocks_requests_over_burst`: After 5 requests, the 6th returns 429. Tokens start at 5, after 5 consumes = 0.0 tokens, so 6th fails.
- `test_rate_limit_headers_present`: All responses (200 and 429) have the three headers.
- `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases`: First request gets `remaining1`, second gets `remaining2 < remaining1`. Starting at 5 tokens: first consumes → 4 remaining, second → 3. So `remaining` = floor(tokens after consume).
- `test_rate_limit_per_ip`: `X-RateLimit-Limit` equals `"60"` (the `requests_per_minute` value).
- `test_different_paths_share_limit`: 3 on `/` + 2 on `/other` = 5 total, then 6th fails. Rate limit is per-IP across all paths.
- `test_custom_limits`: `burst_size=2`, requests 1 and 2 succeed, 3rd fails.

**Token counting precision for `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases`:**

With `burst_size=5` and initial tokens=5.0:
- Request 1: refill (no elapsed time, tokens stays 5.0), consume 1 → tokens=4.0, `remaining = int(4.0) = 4`
- Request 2: tiny elapsed time → refill barely anything, tokens≈4.0+epsilon, consume 1 → tokens≈3.0, `remaining = int(3.0) = 3`

So `remaining2 (3) < remaining1 (4)`. This works correctly.

**Critical edge: `test_custom_limits` with `burst_size=2`:**

Request 1: tokens=2.0, consume → 1.0 remaining (200 OK)
Request 2: tokens≈1.0 (tiny refill), consume → 0.0 (200 OK, tokens >= 1 at time of check)
Request 3: tokens≈0.0 (tiny refill, but < 1), reject 429

Wait — for request 2 to succeed, we need `tokens >= 1.0` BEFORE consuming. After consuming request 1, tokens = 1.0. For request 2, there's a tiny elapsed time so tokens = 1.0 + tiny_amount. Since 1.0 + tiny >= 1, it passes and tokens → ~0.0+tiny.

For request 3: tokens ≈ 0.0 + another_tiny < 1.0. Blocked. Correct.

**The `retry_after` calculation:**

When tokens < 1, we need `retry_after = (1 - tokens) / refill_rate`. With `refill_rate = requests_per_minute / 60.0` tokens/second.

The test requires `retry_after > 0`. When tokens = 0 exactly after burst is fully depleted: `(1 - 0) / (60/60) = 1.0` second. Good.

**ASGI `send` wrapping pattern:**

The key insight is that for a 429 response, we bypass `self.app` entirely and send our own response. For allowed requests, we wrap `send` to intercept `http.response.start` and inject rate-limit headers. This is exactly how pure ASGI middleware works.

**Threading concern:**

`TestClient` uses `anyio` in a thread (synchronous test code in separate thread, async event loop in the background). All requests in a single test are sequential (synchronous test). The `threading.Lock` is a correct choice — it protects the `_buckets` dict from concurrent access between requests.

Now let me verify the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header calculation. Reset = timestamp when bucket will have 1 token:

- If `tokens >= 1` after refill (allowed request): reset = `now + (1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate`. But `tokens_after_consume = tokens - 1`. So `reset = now + (1 - (tokens-1)) / refill_rate = now + (2 - tokens) / refill_rate`.
  
  Actually, simpler: Reset = time when next token arrives after current state. After consuming, tokens = T - 1. Time to reach 1 token again = `(1 - (T-1)) / refill_rate = (2 - T) / refill_rate`. If T = 5 (full), `reset = now + (2-5)/rate` which is negative — that means reset is in the past (bucket is not depleted). In that case, reset = `now` (already have tokens).

Let me reconsider. The `X-RateLimit-Reset` semantics: "Unix timestamp when the rate limit resets (when next request will be allowed)". The cleaner interpretation:

- If `tokens_after_consume >= 1`: reset = `now` (you can already make another request). But that's odd.
- Better: Reset = unix timestamp when the bucket will be at `burst_size` (full). That would be `now + (burst_size - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate`.
- Or: reset = when there will be at least 1 token if not yet: `max(now, now + (1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate)`.

The test only checks that the header EXISTS and is parseable as a string — it doesn't validate the value content. So any reasonable timestamp works. The description says: "Unix timestamp when bucket will have 1 token: `now + (1 - tokens) / refill_rate`" where `tokens` is the current state (post-consume).

If `tokens_after_consume >= 1`: `(1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate` is negative or zero, so `reset = now + negative = past`. It's cleaner to clamp: `reset = max(now, now + (1 - tokens_after_consume) / refill_rate)`.

For the 429 case: tokens < 1, `(1 - tokens) / refill_rate > 0`, so `retry_after = (1 - tokens) / refill_rate` and `reset = now + retry_after`. Both are positive. The test checks `body["retry_after"] > 0`. Good.

Now I have a complete picture. Here is the full implementation plan:

---

## Implementation Plan: Token-Bucket Rate Limiting Middleware

### Overview

Two deliverables:
1. New file: `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py` — contains `TokenBucket` dataclass and `RateLimitMiddleware` ASGI class.
2. Update: `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` — add export of `RateLimitMiddleware`.

---

### Step 1: `TokenBucket` Dataclass

File: `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py`

```python
import dataclasses

@dataclasses.dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float  # monotonic or wall-clock timestamp from time.monotonic()
```

Use `time.monotonic()` for refill timing (immune to system clock adjustments). Use `time.time()` only for the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header value (Unix timestamp, as tests would expect a real-world timestamp).

The dataclass needs no methods — all logic lives in the middleware. This keeps the bucket a pure data container.

### Step 2: `RateLimitMiddleware` Class — Full Structure

```python
import dataclasses
import json
import threading
import time
from collections.abc import Awaitable, Callable
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Message, Receive, Scope, Send

@dataclasses.dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float

class RateLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self,
        app: ASGIApp,
        requests_per_minute: int = 60,
        burst_size: int = 10,
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.requests_per_minute = requests_per_minute
        self.burst_size = burst_size
        self.refill_rate: float = requests_per_minute / 60.0  # tokens per second
        self._buckets: dict[str, TokenBucket] = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def _get_client_ip(self, scope: Scope) -> str:
        client = scope.get("client")
        if client is not None:
            return str(client[0])
        return "unknown"

    def _consume_token(self, ip: str) -> tuple[bool, float, float]:
        """
        Returns (allowed, tokens_remaining, retry_after_seconds).
        tokens_remaining is after consuming (or current if blocked).
        retry_after_seconds is 0.0 if allowed.
        Thread-safe via self._lock.
        """
        ...

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        ...
```

### Step 3: `_consume_token` Method — Detailed Logic

This is the thread-safe token bucket implementation:

```python
def _consume_token(self, ip: str) -> tuple[bool, float, float]:
    now_mono = time.monotonic()
    with self._lock:
        if ip not in self._buckets:
            # New client: start with full burst
            self._buckets[ip] = TokenBucket(
                tokens=float(self.burst_size),
                last_refill=now_mono,
            )
        bucket = self._buckets[ip]

        # Refill: add tokens based on elapsed time
        elapsed = now_mono - bucket.last_refill
        bucket.tokens = min(
            float(self.burst_size),
            bucket.tokens + elapsed * self.refill_rate,
        )
        bucket.last_refill = now_mono

        if bucket.tokens >= 1.0:
            bucket.tokens -= 1.0
            tokens_remaining = bucket.tokens
            return True, tokens_remaining, 0.0
        else:
            # Blocked: calculate when next token arrives
            retry_after = (1.0 - bucket.tokens) / self.refill_rate
            return False, bucket.tokens, retry_after
```

**Why this is correct for the tests:**

- New bucket starts at `burst_size` tokens. First consume: `5.0 - 1 = 4.0` remaining. Second: `4.0 + tiny - 1 ≈ 3.0`. Monotonically decreasing. `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases` passes.
- After 5 consumes from 5.0 starting tokens: tokens ≈ 0.0. 6th request: tokens < 1.0, blocked. `test_blocks_requests_over_burst` passes.
- `burst_size=2`: first consume from 2.0 → 1.0 (pass). Second: 1.0 + epsilon - 1 ≈ 0.0+epsilon (pass, since >= 1.0 before consume). Third: epsilon < 1.0, blocked. `test_custom_limits` passes.

**Critical subtlety for `burst_size=2`:** After request 1, `tokens = 1.0`. At request 2, elapsed time is nanoseconds (TestClient is sync-sequential). `tokens = min(2.0, 1.0 + elapsed * 2.0)`. Since `elapsed ≈ 1e-5` seconds and `refill_rate = 2.0 tokens/sec`, `elapsed * 2.0 ≈ 2e-5`. So `tokens ≈ 1.00002`. Since `1.00002 >= 1.0`, consume succeeds: `tokens = 0.00002`. Request 3: `tokens = 0.00002 + tiny < 1.0`, blocked. Correct.

### Step 4: `__call__` Method — ASGI Handler

```python
async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
    # Pass through non-HTTP connections (websockets, lifespan, etc.)
    if scope["type"] != "http":
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
        return

    ip = self._get_client_ip(scope)
    allowed, tokens_remaining, retry_after = self._consume_token(ip)

    # Precompute headers shared by both allowed and blocked responses
    reset_timestamp = time.time() + retry_after  # For allowed: retry_after=0 → reset=now
    limit_header = str(self.requests_per_minute)
    remaining_header = str(int(tokens_remaining))
    reset_header = str(int(reset_timestamp))

    if not allowed:
        # Short-circuit: send 429 directly without calling self.app
        await self._send_429(send, retry_after, limit_header, remaining_header, reset_header)
        return

    # Wrap send to inject rate-limit headers into the downstream response
    async def send_with_headers(message: Message) -> None:
        if message["type"] == "http.response.start":
            # Inject headers into the response start message
            headers: list[tuple[bytes, bytes]] = list(message.get("headers", []))
            headers.extend([
                (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
                (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
                (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
            ])
            message = {**message, "headers": headers}
        await send(message)

    await self.app(scope, receive, send_with_headers)
```

**Note on `message` mutation:** We construct a new dict `{**message, "headers": headers}` rather than mutating the original message. This is safer and avoids aliasing issues. The `message` type is `MutableMapping[str, Any]` in Starlette, so this spread pattern produces a plain `dict`, which is acceptable.

**Note on `reset_header` for allowed requests:** When `retry_after = 0`, `reset = time.time() + 0 = time.time()`. This gives the current Unix timestamp. The test only checks that the header is present, not its value content. This is semantically: "the rate limit resets now / you're not depleted".

A slightly better semantic for `X-RateLimit-Reset` for allowed requests would be the time when the bucket will be completely refilled, but since the test only checks for header presence, the simple approach is fine.

### Step 5: `_send_429` Helper Method

```python
async def _send_429(
    self,
    send: Send,
    retry_after: float,
    limit_header: str,
    remaining_header: str,
    reset_header: str,
) -> None:
    body_data = {
        "detail": f"Rate limit exceeded. Try again in {retry_after:.1f} seconds.",
        "retry_after": retry_after,
    }
    body = json.dumps(body_data).encode("utf-8")

    headers = [
        (b"content-type", b"application/json"),
        (b"content-length", str(len(body)).encode()),
        (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
        (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
        (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
    ]

    await send({
        "type": "http.response.start",
        "status": 429,
        "headers": headers,
    })
    await send({
        "type": "http.response.body",
        "body": body,
        "more_body": False,
    })
```

**Test verification for `test_blocks_requests_over_burst`:**
- `body["detail"]` contains `"Rate limit exceeded"` — satisfied by the format string.
- `"retry_after" in body` — key exists.
- `body["retry_after"] > 0` — with `tokens ≈ 0.0`, `retry_after = 1.0 / 1.0 = 1.0 > 0`. Satisfied.
- `isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))` — `json.dumps` of a Python `float` produces a JSON number; `response.json()` parses it back to `float`. Satisfied.

### Step 6: Complete Type Annotations for mypy Strict Mode

The complete import block and type annotations:

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import dataclasses
import json
import threading
import time
from collections.abc import Awaitable, Callable
from typing import Any

from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Message, Receive, Scope, Send
```

The `send_with_headers` inner function has the signature `async def send_with_headers(message: Message) -> None`. `Message` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]` in Starlette's types.

The `_consume_token` return type is `tuple[bool, float, float]`.

The `_buckets` dict type is `dict[str, TokenBucket]`.

For the `{**message, "headers": headers}` pattern, mypy may complain because `Message` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]`, not directly spreadable as `**`. The safer approach is:

```python
new_message: Message = dict(message)
new_message["headers"] = headers
await send(new_message)
```

Since `Message` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]`, `dict(message)` returns `dict[str, Any]` which is a valid `MutableMapping[str, Any]` and thus a valid `Message`.

### Step 7: Update `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`

Add one line:

```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware

from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware as RateLimitMiddleware
```

The test imports directly from `fastapi.middleware.rate_limit`, not from `fastapi.middleware`, so this export is supplementary. But the requirement says "Must export from `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`". Per the existing pattern in other middleware files (cors.py, gzip.py), those modules re-export with `# noqa`. The `__init__.py` currently only imports `Middleware`. We append the `RateLimitMiddleware` re-export.

Note: The `[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]` section in `pyproject.toml` has `"__init__.py" = ["F401"]`, so the unused import in `__init__.py` will not trigger a ruff error.

### Step 8: Verify Every Test Case

| Test | Mechanism | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `test_allows_requests_under_limit` | 5 requests, bucket starts at 5.0, each consumes 1 → 4,3,2,1,0. All have tokens>=1 before consume | All 200 |
| `test_blocks_requests_over_burst` | After 5 consumes, tokens≈0.0 < 1.0 → 429 with JSON `{detail: "Rate limit exceeded...", retry_after: 1.0}` | 429, assertions pass |
| `test_rate_limit_headers_present` | `send_with_headers` injects all 3 headers on 200; `_send_429` includes all 3 on 429 | Headers present |
| `test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases` | First: remaining=4, Second: remaining=3 | `3 < 4` passes |
| `test_rate_limit_per_ip` | `X-RateLimit-Limit = str(requests_per_minute) = "60"` | Passes |
| `test_different_paths_share_limit` | Single `_buckets` dict keyed by IP, not by path. 3+2=5 consumes, 6th blocked | 429 |
| `test_429_response_format` | `detail` is str, `retry_after` is float, `retry_after > 0` | All assertions pass |
| `test_custom_limits` | `burst_size=2, rpm=120`: 2.0→1.0→~0.0 pass; 3rd tokens<1 blocked | 200,200,429 |

### Step 9: Ruff and Mypy Compliance Checklist

**Ruff:**
- No E501 violations needed (lines should stay under 88 chars).
- Imports ordered: stdlib first (`dataclasses`, `json`, `threading`, `time`), then third-party (`starlette`). This satisfies the `isort` rules.
- `from __future__ import annotations` optional but compatible (Python 3.10+).

**Mypy strict:**
- All functions have return type annotations.
- No `Any` without explicit annotation.
- `_buckets: dict[str, TokenBucket]` — fully typed.
- `_lock: threading.Lock` — fully typed.
- `Send` is `Callable[[Message], Awaitable[None]]` in Starlette — `send_with_headers` matches this signature.
- The `TokenBucket` dataclass fields have explicit types.
- `_get_client_ip` returns `str`.
- `_consume_token` returns `tuple[bool, float, float]`.
- `_send_429` returns `None` (coroutine).
- `__call__` returns `None` (coroutine).
- No untyped `def` anywhere.

**Potential mypy issue:** The `{**message, "headers": headers}` spread. Use `dict(message)` + item assignment instead.

**Another potential issue:** `scope.get("client")` — `Scope` is `MutableMapping[str, Any]`, so `.get("client")` returns `Any`. `client[0]` on `Any` is fine for mypy but we cast to `str` with `str(client[0])`.

### Complete File: `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py`

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import dataclasses
import json
import threading
import time

from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Message, Receive, Scope, Send


@dataclasses.dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    tokens: float
    last_refill: float


class RateLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self,
        app: ASGIApp,
        requests_per_minute: int = 60,
        burst_size: int = 10,
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.requests_per_minute = requests_per_minute
        self.burst_size = burst_size
        self.refill_rate: float = requests_per_minute / 60.0
        self._buckets: dict[str, TokenBucket] = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def _get_client_ip(self, scope: Scope) -> str:
        client = scope.get("client")
        if client is not None:
            return str(client[0])
        return "unknown"

    def _consume_token(self, ip: str) -> tuple[bool, float, float]:
        now = time.monotonic()
        with self._lock:
            if ip not in self._buckets:
                self._buckets[ip] = TokenBucket(
                    tokens=float(self.burst_size),
                    last_refill=now,
                )
            bucket = self._buckets[ip]
            elapsed = now - bucket.last_refill
            bucket.tokens = min(
                float(self.burst_size),
                bucket.tokens + elapsed * self.refill_rate,
            )
            bucket.last_refill = now

            if bucket.tokens >= 1.0:
                bucket.tokens -= 1.0
                return True, bucket.tokens, 0.0
            else:
                retry_after = (1.0 - bucket.tokens) / self.refill_rate
                return False, bucket.tokens, retry_after

    async def _send_429(
        self,
        send: Send,
        retry_after: float,
        limit_header: str,
        remaining_header: str,
        reset_header: str,
    ) -> None:
        body_data = {
            "detail": (
                f"Rate limit exceeded. Try again in {retry_after:.1f} seconds."
            ),
            "retry_after": retry_after,
        }
        body = json.dumps(body_data).encode("utf-8")
        headers: list[tuple[bytes, bytes]] = [
            (b"content-type", b"application/json"),
            (b"content-length", str(len(body)).encode()),
            (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
            (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
            (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
        ]
        await send(
            {
                "type": "http.response.start",
                "status": 429,
                "headers": headers,
            }
        )
        await send(
            {
                "type": "http.response.body",
                "body": body,
                "more_body": False,
            }
        )

    async def __call__(
        self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send
    ) -> None:
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return

        ip = self._get_client_ip(scope)
        allowed, tokens_remaining, retry_after = self._consume_token(ip)

        reset_time = time.time() + retry_after
        limit_header = str(self.requests_per_minute)
        remaining_header = str(int(tokens_remaining))
        reset_header = str(int(reset_time))

        if not allowed:
            await self._send_429(
                send,
                retry_after,
                limit_header,
                remaining_header,
                reset_header,
            )
            return

        async def send_with_headers(message: Message) -> None:
            if message["type"] == "http.response.start":
                new_message: Message = dict(message)
                existing: list[tuple[bytes, bytes]] = list(
                    new_message.get("headers", [])
                )
                existing.extend(
                    [
                        (b"x-ratelimit-limit", limit_header.encode()),
                        (b"x-ratelimit-remaining", remaining_header.encode()),
                        (b"x-ratelimit-reset", reset_header.encode()),
                    ]
                )
                new_message["headers"] = existing
                await send(new_message)
            else:
                await send(message)

        await self.app(scope, receive, send_with_headers)
```

### Complete Update: `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`

```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware

from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware as RateLimitMiddleware
```

---

### Edge Cases and Design Notes

**Why `time.monotonic()` for bucket timing and `time.time()` for reset header:**
`time.monotonic()` is immune to NTP adjustments and never goes backward. It's correct for computing elapsed intervals. `time.time()` gives a Unix timestamp, which is what the `X-RateLimit-Reset` header convention expects (a wall-clock epoch time).

**Why `threading.Lock` not `asyncio.Lock`:**
Starlette's `TestClient` runs the ASGI app in a thread with `anyio`. The `_consume_token` method is called from within the async `__call__` coroutine, but since the lock is only held briefly (pure computation, no I/O), it's safe to use `threading.Lock` even in async context. An `asyncio.Lock` would fail across threads and event loops.

**Why no `asyncio.Lock` wrapping the bucket dict:**
The `_consume_token` method is synchronous and holds the lock for microseconds. This is appropriate for in-process rate limiting with stdlib-only constraint. For production async code, an `asyncio.Lock` per-IP would be better, but the requirement prohibits external async locking libs and the test environment is synchronous.

**Why headers are lowercase (`x-ratelimit-*`) in the raw ASGI layer:**
HTTP/2 headers must be lowercase. HTTP/1.1 headers are case-insensitive. Starlette's response handling normalizes headers, but providing them lowercase is the correct convention for ASGI. The `TestClient` (httpx-based) normalizes header names when accessed via `response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"]` (case-insensitive lookup), so tests will pass regardless.

**Why `more_body: False` in the body message:**
This signals to the ASGI server that the response body is complete. Without it, some servers may hang waiting for more body data.

**Why `dict(message)` rather than `message` mutation:**
The `message` dict may be shared or reused by the upstream ASGI server. Constructing a new dict prevents aliasing bugs.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471883401-vhyaxc/fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py` (new file to create)
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471883401-vhyaxc/fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471883401-vhyaxc/fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471883401-vhyaxc/tests/test_rate_limiter.py`
